table.evidence:
parameter: Cross-Origin-Resource-Policy
description: Cross-Origin-Resource-Policy header is an opt-in header designed to counter side-channels attacks like Spectre. Resource should be specifically set as shareable amongst different origins.
table.suggested_mitigation: Ensure that the application/web server sets the Cross-Origin-Resource-Policy header appropriately, and that it sets the Cross-Origin-Resource-Policy header to 'same-origin' for all web pages.'same-site' is considered as less secu...

table.evidence:
parameter: x-content-type-options
other_info: This issue still applies to error type pages (401, 403, 500, etc.) as those pages are often still affected by injection issues, in which case there is still concern for browsers sniffing pages away from their actual content type.At "High" threshold this scan rule will not alert on client or server error responses.
description: The Anti-MIME-Sniffing header X-Content-Type-Options was not set to 'nosniff'. This allows older versions...
table.suggested_mitigation: Ensure X-Content-Type-Options header is set to 'nosniff'.

table.vulnerability: A Client Error response code was returned by the server
table.evidence:
evidence: 404
description: A response code of 404 was returned by the server.This may indicate that the application is failing to handle unexpected input correctly.Raised by the 'Alert on HTTP Response Code Error' script
table.suggested_mitigation: Ensure the application handles unexpected input gracefully and returns appropriate response codes.

table.evidence:
evidence: DELETE 
description: The response contents are not storable by caching components such as proxy servers. If the response does not contain sensitive, personal or user-specific information, it may benefit from being stored and cached, to improve performance.
table.suggested_mitigation: The content may be marked as storable by ensuring that the following conditions are satisfied:The request method must be understood by the cache and defined as being cacheable ("GET", "HEAD", and "POST" ...)

table.evidence:
parameter: Sec-Fetch-Dest
description: Specifies how and where the data would be used. For instance, if the value is audio, then the requested resource must be audio data and not any other type of resource.
table.suggested_mitigation: Ensure that Sec-Fetch-Dest header is included in request headers.

table.evidence:
parameter: Sec-Fetch-Mode
description: Allows to differentiate between requests for navigating between HTML pages and requests for loading resources like images, audio etc.
table.suggested_mitigation: Ensure that Sec-Fetch-Mode header is included in request headers.

table.evidence:
parameter: Sec-Fetch-Site
description: Specifies the relationship between request initiator's origin and target's origin.
table.suggested_mitigation: Ensure that Sec-Fetch-Site header is included in request headers.

table.evidence:
parameter: Sec-Fetch-User
description: Specifies if a navigation request was initiated by a user.
table.suggested_mitigation: Ensure that Sec-Fetch-User header is included in user initiated requests.

table.evidence:
other_info: In the absence of an explicitly specified caching lifetime directive in the response, a liberal lifetime heuristic of 1 year was assumed. This is permitted by rfc7234.
description: The response contents are storable by caching components such as proxy servers, and may be retrieved directly from the cache, rather than from the origin server by the caching servers, in response to similar requests from other users. If the response data is sensitive, personal or user-specifi...
table.suggested_mitigation: If the response data is sensitive, personal or user-specific, ensure appropriate caching headers (e.g., Cache-Control: no-store, private) are set to prevent caching by proxy servers.

table.vulnerability: Content Security Policy (CSP) Header Not Set
table.evidence: description: Content Security Policy (CSP) is an added layer of security that helps to detect and mitigate certain types of attacks, including Cross Site Scripting (XSS) and data injection attacks. These attacks are used for everything from data theft to site defacement or distribution of malware. CSP provides a set of standard HTTP headers that allow website owners to declare approved sources of content that browsers should be allowed to load on that page - covered types are JavaScript, C...
table.suggested_mitigation: Implement a Content Security Policy (CSP) header to declare approved sources of content and mitigate XSS and data injection attacks.

table.evidence:
parameter: x-frame-options
description: The response does not protect against 'ClickJacking' attacks. It should include either Content-Security-Policy with 'frame-ancestors' directive or X-Frame-Options.
table.suggested_mitigation: Modern Web browsers support the Content-Security-Policy and X-Frame-Options HTTP headers. Ensure one of them is set on all web pages returned by your site/app.If you expect the page to be framed only by pages on your server (e.g. it's part of a FRAMESET) then you'll...

table.evidence:
parameter: Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy
description: Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy header is a response header that prevents a document from loading any cross-origin resources that don't explicitly grant the document permission (using CORP or CORS).
table.suggested_mitigation: Ensure that the application/web server sets the Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy header appropriately, and that it sets the Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy header to 'require-corp' for documents.If possible, ensure that the end ...

table.evidence:
parameter: Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy
description: Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy header is a response header that allows a site to control if others included documents share the same browsing context. Sharing the same browsing context with untrusted documents might lead to data leak.
table.suggested_mitigation: Ensure that the application/web server sets the Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy header appropriately, and that it sets the Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy header to 'same-origin' for documents.'same...

table.evidence: description: Permissions Policy Header is an added layer of security that helps to restrict from unauthorized access or usage of browser/client features by web resources. This policy ensures the user privacy by limiting or specifying the features of the browsers can be used by the web resources. Permissions Policy provides a set of standard HTTP headers that allow website owners to limit which features of browsers can be used by the page such as camera, microphone, location, full screen et...
table.suggested_mitigation: Implement a Permissions Policy Header to restrict unauthorized access or usage of browser/client features by web resources.

table.vulnerability: Server Leaks Version Information via "Server" HTTP Response Header Field
table.evidence:
evidence: nginx/1.27.5
description: The web/application server is leaking version information via the "Server" HTTP response header. Access to such information may facilitate attackers identifying other vulnerabilities your web/application server is subject to.
table.suggested_mitigation: Ensure that your web server, application server, load balancer, etc. is configured to suppress the "Server" header or provide generic details.

table.suggested_mitigation: Ensure X-Content-Type-Options header is set to 'nosniff'.

table.evidence:
parameter: Header User-Agent
attack: Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 10.0; Trident/7.0; rv:11.0) like Gecko
description: Check for differences in response based on fuzzed User Agent (eg. mobile sites, access as a Search Engine Crawler). Compares the response statuscode and the hashcode of the response body with the original response.
table.suggested_mitigation: This is an informational alert. Review if different responses based on User-Agent are expected and handled securely.

table.evidence: request: {"body": null, "headers": {"Accept": "*/*", "Accept-Encoding": "gzip, deflate", "Connection": "keep-alive", "User-Agent": "schemathesis/4.14.2", "X-Schemathesis-Probe": "Supports NULL byte in headers", "X-Schemathesis-Probe-Null": "\u0000"}, "method": "GET", "url": "http://host.docker.internal:8080/"}
table.suggested_mitigation: Ensure that the server correctly handles and sanitizes HTTP headers, specifically rejecting or properly encoding NULL bytes to prevent parsing ambiguities or injection attacks.

notes: ZAP confidence levels (1, 2, 3) have been mapped to 'low', 'medium', 'high' respectively.

For ZAP findings, 'suggested_mitigation' is extracted from the 'solution' field if available, otherwise inferred from the 'description'. HTML tags like

and

have been removed from evidence and mitigation strings.
For Schemathesis findings, 'severity', 'threat_model_relation', and 'suggested_mitigation' have been inferred based on the nature of the 'Supports NULL byte in headers' vulnerability.
